titleStmt.title: The Impact of Review Copies on German Online Book Reviews from LovelyBooks

keywords.term: I plan to attend the conference on-site in Lisbon

div.head: Introduction

p.hi: Lay book reviews are of high importance for all stakeholders in the book market: For their readers, they are a valuable tool to navigate the high variety of books, considering alone about 67,000 newly published books in Germany 2023 (Börsenverein des deutschen Buchhandels 2024). For their writers, reviews are a way to connect to other readers. For publishers and authors, they constitute an effective marketing strategy to foster book sales because reviews are deemed more credible than traditional marketing strategies (e.g. Schmidt 2018), such as advertising banners. Consequently, cooperations with online book reviewers are well established. Often, review copies are given to reviewers in exchange for a review.
In Germany, incentivised book reviews are a form of advertising and must be disclosed (die medienanstalten 2019

). Conversely, this research is based on the observation that reviewers often do not comply with these restrictions. Instead, cooperations are disclosed implicitly by expressing gratitude for having received a free copy, sometimes by emphasising their objectivity, e.g.: “Many thanks for providing the review copy. This has no impact on my opinion.”

div.p: Although incentivised reviews have been subject to many studies, a well-founded investigation targeted on German book reviews is still lacking.

p.hi: The research presented in this poster aims at filling this gap by questioning book reviewers’ self-stated objectivity. The goal consists in investigating the influence of incentivisation on book reviews. To do so, a dataset of German online book reviews from the social cataloguing site LovelyBooks was analysed (first introduced by Rebora et al. 2022).

Two samples of incentivised (IRs) and non-incentivised reviews (NIRs) were drawn. In line with other studies (e.g. Costa et al. 2019, Hu et al. 2023), IRs were identified by assuming that reviewers explicitly disclose the cooperation by expressing their gratitude. Seven deterministic terms with >90% accuracy were used for IR identification, e.g. “Rezensionsexemplar” (review copy, 98.96% accuracy). Conversely, NIRs were identified by assuming that a review is not incentivised, if it does not contain any of the terms from the IR detection approach (in line with e.g. Hu et al. 2023). Overall, two samples of 4872 reviews were used. To avoid possible biases, a single genre was selected: romance, because it had the highest absolute number of IRs. As the NIR sample was much larger than the IR sample (approx. 125,000 reviews), NIRs were selected randomly.

The concept of “influence” was measured across five dimensions (derived from Kim et al. 2019): positivity, complexity, elaborateness, extremeness and objectivity. Rule-based proxies were chosen to operationalise and formalise the concepts, e.g. Sentiment Analysis using sentiArt (Jacobs 2019) to measure a review’s positivity. Statistical tests were conducted to compare the outcome.

No differences were found in terms of positivity, complexity, extremeness, and objectivity. Only in case of review elaborateness–measured by considering review length as a proxy–, a significant difference was found: IRs are more elaborate than NIRs. Consequently, book reviews show only few signs of influence if the reviewer received a review copy. In other words: incentivised book reviewers and non-incentivised reviewers indeed seem to be equally objective.
A web-based presentation is also available (Heumann 2024).

hi.hi: The regulations from 2018 were considered because the analysed reviews were published before 2019.

(2019): “Unfolding the characteristics of incentivized online reviews”, in:
Journal of Retailing and Consumer Services
, ed. (2019): “Leitfaden der Medienanstalten – Werbekennzeichnung bei Online-Medien 2018”. Published online January 23, 2019. <

(2024): “Incentivized Book Reviews.” Web-based presentation of “The Impact of Review Copies on German Online Book Reviews from LovelyBooks”. Unpublished master’s thesis, University of Mainz / Hochschule Mainz. <
